authorship_evidence: Repository created in 2021, pre-LLM era, educational project with consistent human-written commit history and comments

evidence.description: Arbitrary file read via path traversal. self.path from the HTTP request is concatenated to cwd at line 14 and opened at line 22 via open(path).read(). The regex filter on line 10-11 (r'/\.\.\/\.\./' with re.match) is trivially bypassable — it only matches a very specific literal pattern at the start of the path and fails for encodings, alternate traversal sequences, or simple variations like '/../etc/passwd'.

SQL injection via string concatenation: conn.execute('SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id = '+post_id). The post_id parameter is concatenated directly into the SQL query. The regex filter on line 67-68 (r'^\d+$' with re.M) is weak because the re.M flag makes ^ and $ match line boundaries, allowing multiline input like '1\nOR 1=1' to pass the filter.

Hardcoded Flask secret key: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = 'qwerty12345'. This weak, predictable secret key allows attackers to forge session cookies and CSRF tokens, potentially leading to session hijacking.
Hardcoded Flask secret key: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = '55555'. This trivially weak secret key allows attackers to forge session cookies.
SQL injection via string concatenation: cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE username = '" + username + '"'). User-supplied username is concatenated directly into the SQL query string.
SQL injection via str.format(): cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE username = '{}'".format(username)). User-supplied username is interpolated into SQL via Python's format() method.
SQL injection via %-formatting: cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE username = '%s' % username). User-supplied username is interpolated into SQL via Python's % string formatting operator.
SQL injection via f-string: cursor.execute(f"SELECT ... WHERE username = '{username}'"). User-supplied username is interpolated into SQL via Python f-string.

XSS via unescaped output. The username variable (derived from decrypting a user-supplied cookie at line 26) is rendered into HTML without any escaping: bytes('Hello %s' % username, 'utf-8'). An attacker who controls the encrypted cookie value can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript.

Insecure deserialization via pickle. The Username.pickle() call on line 14 deserializes user-controlled cookie data using pickle (as documented in the commented-out code on line 15: pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(cookies.get('username').value))). Pickle deserialization of untrusted data allows arbitrary code execution via crafted __reduce__ methods.

SSRF via urllib.request.urlopen(url). The url parameter comes from request.args.get('url'). The startswith('https://insecurewebapp.com') check on line 14 is trivially bypassable by using URLs like 'https://insecurewebapp.com.attacker.com/' or 'https://insecurewebapp.com@attacker.com/', allowing the server to fetch arbitrary internal or external URLs.

Vulnerable to hash length extension attack. Uses SHA256(secret + payment_information) instead of HMAC for signing payment data. The naive secret-prefix construction allows attackers to append additional data and compute a valid signature without knowing the secret, enabling payment amount tampering.

Vulnerable to hash length extension attack. Uses SHA256(secret + payment_information) instead of HMAC for signing payment data. Same vulnerability as length_extension_attack.py — attackers can extend the signed message and forge valid signatures to modify payment amounts.

SSL/TLS hostname verification disabled: context.check_hostname = False. This allows man-in-the-middle attacks by accepting any certificate regardless of the hostname it was issued for, enabling interception of encrypted traffic.

Bare except clause catches all exceptions (including padding errors from AES-CBC decryption) and returns HTTP 500 without logging. While this prevents stack trace exposure, the error response timing differences in AES-CBC decryption with unpad() can leak information about padding validity, potentially enabling a padding oracle attack (CWE-209).

Pickle deserialization exploit PoC demonstrating RCE. The __reduce__ method returns os.system with a reverse shell command, and pickle.dumps() at line 17 serializes this payload. While this is an exploit tool rather than a vulnerable endpoint, it demonstrates the insecure deserialization attack vector using pickle's __reduce__ protocol.

Reflected XSS via unescaped username in HTML response. The username variable (derived from deserializing a user-controlled cookie) is interpolated directly into an HTML response at line 30 using string formatting ('Hello %s' % username) without any HTML escaping. This is a distinct vulnerability from the pickle deserialization at line 14 (GT-010) — it represents a separate XSS sink. An attacker who controls the cookie value can inject arbitrary JavaScript into the response.

Missing authentication on the create() handler (lines 93-109). The handler accepts POST requests to create new blog posts with no authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can insert arbitrary content into the database. Note: the scanner incorrectly labeled this as the 'edit()' handler, but lines 93-109 correspond to the create() function. The actual edit() handler starts at line 112 and also lacks authentication.

AES-CBC padding oracle vulnerability in the decrypt() method. The method at lines 15-20 uses AES-CBC mode with unpad() on decrypted data. When padding is invalid, the exception propagates to the bare except at lines 27-29 which returns HTTP 500, while valid padding returns HTTP 200. This error differential creates a classic padding oracle that allows an attacker to decrypt any ciphertext block-by-block without knowing the key, and potentially forge valid ciphertexts. Distinct from GT-015 which covers the error handler side (lines 27-29); this entry covers the vulnerable crypto implementation itself.

Hardcoded secret value at line 7 used as the signing key for payment signatures. Anyone with source code access can recover the secret and forge valid payment signatures, regardless of the hash construction used. Distinct from GT-012 which covers the weak SHA256 hash construction (CWE-328) at lines 17-18; this entry covers the hardcoded secret itself.

Hardcoded secret value at line 5 used as the signing key for payment signatures. Anyone with source code access can recover the secret and forge valid payment signatures. Distinct from GT-013 which covers the weak SHA256 hash construction (CWE-328) at lines 12-13; this entry covers the hardcoded secret itself.

Missing CSRF protection on the Flask payment application. The /redirect_for_payment endpoint handles payment-related operations without CSRF tokens. An attacker can craft a cross-origin request that triggers payment operations using an authenticated victim's session. No Flask-WTF or other CSRF middleware is configured.

Safe parameterized query: conn.execute('SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id = ?', (post_id,)). Uses ? placeholder with tuple parameter binding, which prevents SQL injection. This is the first (safe) version of get_post, before the second (vulnerable) get_db_connection redefinition.
Safe parameterized query: conn.execute('SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id = ?', (post_id,)). This is the safe version of the SQLI app, using proper parameter binding to prevent SQL injection.

Safe parameterized INSERT: conn.execute('INSERT INTO posts (title, content) VALUES (?, ?)', (title, content)). Uses ? placeholders with tuple parameter binding. User-supplied title and content from request.form are safely bound as parameters.

Safe autoescaped output. The template uses {% autoescape true %} (line 3) and renders comments via {{ comment }} (line 35), which applies HTML escaping. Despite being labeled as an 'XSS vulnerable web app' in app.py docstring, the current template code is safe — Jinja2 autoescaping converts <, >, &, and quotes to HTML entities.

Safe parameterized INSERT: db.cursor().execute('INSERT INTO comments (comment) VALUES (?)', (comment,)). The user-supplied comment is safely bound via ? placeholder parameter binding, preventing SQL injection.
